content.text: Decoding methods define
how

the next token is chosen from model logits: greedy argmax, beam search, or stochastic sampling. For agents, greedy and low-temperature sampling dominate production APIs; beam search appears in translation and some batch inference pipelines but rarely in interactive tool-calling agents.
What it is
Greedy decoding
selects the highest-probability token at each step.
Beam search

maintains b partial hypotheses, expanding the top candidates each step and keeping the b highest-scoring sequences by cumulative log-probability. Both are deterministic (given fixed model weights); sampling adds randomness on top.
Why it matters

Greedy is fast—one forward pass per token—and maps cleanly to streaming APIs. Beam search can improve global coherence for short outputs but costs b× compute per step and is poorly suited to long agent traces with tool calls. Choosing the wrong method affects latency budgets and whether the model "commits" to a suboptimal early token.
How it works
Greedy decoding
At each step: compute logits → softmax → pick argmax token → append → repeat until stop. Equivalent to temperature → 0 with no top-p restriction.
Beam search

Maintain a beam of b sequences. Each step, expand each sequence with top-k next tokens, score by sum of log-probabilities (often length-normalized), prune to b best. Used when a single greedy path misses better global strings—for example, short captions or constrained templates.
Benefits
Greedy: minimal latency, simple streaming, default for chat APIs
Beam search: can recover from locally suboptimal first tokens on short outputs
Deterministic decoding enables regression testing with fixed seeds where supported
Trade-offs
Greedy can repeat phrases or stall; no look-ahead
Beam search scales poorly with output length and beam width
Beam search does not integrate cleanly with tool-call interrupts mid-stream
Length normalization and diverse beam variants add implementation complexity
When to use

Use greedy or near-greedy (temperature 0) for agent tool calls, code, and JSON. Consider beam search offline for batch summarization or caption generation—not for multi-turn agents with external tool latency. For open-ended chat, stochastic sampling usually beats wide beams on quality per dollar.
Agentic AI connection

Agent loops interleave generation with tool execution; decoding must stop at delimiters and resume after tool results. Beam search across tool boundaries is impractical. Production agents rely on greedy or sampled decoding plus
validators
(JSON schema, retry prompts) rather than exponential search in token space.
Common mistakes
Enabling beam search in APIs that do not support it mid-agent-loop
Expecting beam search to fix hallucinations—it optimizes probability, not truth
Using wide beams on long chain-of-thought outputs, blowing latency and cost
Treating greedy output as "ground truth" without logprob or external verification
Best practices
Default agent paths to temperature 0 or greedy for structured steps
If exploring alternatives, rerank N sampled completions instead of large beams
Measure end-to-end agent success, not BLEU-style metrics tuned for beam search
Use constrained decoding (grammar, JSON mode) where available instead of beam width
Key takeaways
Greedy picks the top token each step; beam search keeps multiple hypotheses
Interactive agents almost always use greedy or low-temperature sampling
Beam search trades compute for better short-sequence global scores
Validators and retries outperform wide beams for tool-calling reliability
